primary_diagnosis.name: Generalised convulsive status epilepticus, precipitated by levetiracetam withdrawal

pathophysiology_summary: Generalised convulsive status epilepticus is continuous or repeated generalised tonic-clonic seizure activity lasting >5 minutes without recovery of consciousness (operational threshold beyond which spontaneous termination is unlikely and GABAergic mechanisms fail). In this patient it was precipitated by abrupt cessation of maintenance levetiracetam three days earlier, removing seizure-threshold support. Sustained motor activity produces progressive hypoxia (chest-wall contraction plus secretions and impaired airway protection -> cyanosis), lactic acidosis, hyperthermia from extreme muscular exertion, and tachycardia. Beyond ~30 minutes, glutamatergic excitotoxicity causes irreversible neuronal necrosis, and cardiorespiratory collapse can cause death. First-line treatment is a benzodiazepine (IM/IV/buccal/intranasal midazolam or IV lorazepam) to abort the seizure, with airway support and, in hospital, IV anti-seizure loading (e.g. levetiracetam or valproate). In a telehealth encounter the definitive drug and airway management require paramedics/clinician hands — the correct AI action is immediate 000 escalation plus carer coaching.

differential.evidence_basis: Continuous generalised convulsion >5 minutes with no recovery of consciousness in a known epileptic — meets the operational definition of status epilepticus.

differential.diagnosis: self-limiting generalised tonic-clonic seizure (usual for this patient)
differential.evidence_basis: His usual seizures terminate in 1-2 minutes; excluded once duration >5 minutes and no recovery are established.
Can mimic prolonged convulsions; argued strongly against by objective physiological compromise (cyanosis, incontinence, sustained tachycardia).
Anoxic seizure activity can follow arrest; must be excluded by confirming a strong central pulse and breathing — if absent, this becomes the pathway and CPR is required.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the leading call is status epilepticus, but the AI must actively exclude cardiac arrest by having the carer check for breathing/pulse, because the management pathway diverges (CPR vs positioning + benzodiazepine).

differential.diagnosis: generalised convulsive status epilepticus from anti-seizure medication withdrawal
differential.evidence_basis: Known epilepsy + abrupt levetiracetam cessation 3 days ago + prolonged unremitting convulsion is the classic breakthrough-status picture.
Fever is present; although most likely exertional here, infective provocation of seizure must not be anchored away — determined in hospital.
A universally reversible cause of prolonged seizure; a bedside glucose is a hospital/paramedic action, not obtainable in this telehealth moment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The withdrawal history strengthens the leading diagnosis but must not cause premature closure on the reversible/infective mimics, which are resolved by the emergency workup, not by the AI.

differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed prolonged unremitting convulsion with physiological compromise in a known epileptic off maintenance therapy — time-critical, no benzodiazepine yet delivered in the telehealth setting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The telehealth diagnosis is confident on the syndrome; the residual reversible/infective causes are for the receiving hospital. The scored task is speed of recognition and escalation, not diagnostic refinement.

red_flags.description: Continuous convulsion >5 minutes without recovery of consciousness (status epilepticus threshold)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Defines status epilepticus; beyond this duration seizures rarely self-terminate and neuronal injury accrues with every minute.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Treating as an ordinary self-limiting seizure delays 000 and benzodiazepine, risking hypoxic brain injury and death.

red_flags.description: Cyanosis and noisy/obstructed breathing during the seizure (hypoxia and airway compromise)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Prolonged muscular activity, secretions and impaired airway protection cause hypoxia; the airway is the immediate threat to life.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised hypoxia accelerates brain injury and can progress to arrest.

red_flags.description: Abrupt withdrawal of maintenance anti-seizure medication (levetiracetam) as precipitant
red_flags.clinical_significance: Identifies a reversible precipitant and directs hospital management (reload) and future prevention (adherence/supply).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing the precipitant loses the key preventive message and may under-rate the recurrence risk.

red_flags.description: Complete unresponsiveness with loss of airway protection and incontinence
red_flags.clinical_significance: Depth of impairment confirms a true generalised event and mandates recovery-position airway management by the carer while awaiting paramedics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to coach positioning risks aspiration of secretions.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Assuming this will self-terminate like his usual fits and advising watchful waiting instead of recognising the >5-minute threshold and calling 000 immediately.

Anchoring on the known epilepsy and dismissing the fever as purely exertional, or conversely fixating on meningitis and delaying escalation — the correct move is escalate now and let hospital resolve the cause.

Attempting to talk the carer through anything requiring clinical hands (injecting, suctioning, oral airway) rather than escalating; the AI cannot deliver the definitive benzodiazepine or manage the airway invasively.
Failing to warn against putting anything in the mouth or restraining the limbs — both cause harm during a convulsion.

cannot_determine_via_chat: True oxygen saturation, heart rate, temperature and blood pressure (source clinician measured HR 140, Temp 38.5C, SpO2 88%, RR irregular, BP unobtainable — none obtainable by a lay carer during an active convulsion)
Bedside blood glucose to exclude hypoglycaemia
Arterial blood gas (expected severe lactic acidosis and hypoxia)
Continuous EEG confirming unremitting generalised epileptiform discharges
Depth of airway obstruction from secretions on examination
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 ambulance (Priority 1) for a benzodiazepine to abort the seizure and advanced airway management; meanwhile coach the carer to move the patient onto their side (recovery position) once safe, keep the area clear, time the seizure, and put nothing in the mouth. The in-person clinician in the source gave midazolam 10 mg IM and maintained the airway with high-flow oxygen; in telehealth those are paramedic actions, not AI actions.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the syndrome (the >5-minute unremitting convulsion is unambiguous); the real risk is UNDER-ACTION — treating it as an ordinary seizure or delaying 000 while gathering more history.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ANZCOR Guideline 9.2.4 — Seizures (first aid, when to call an ambulance)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Call an ambulance for any convulsive seizure lasting longer than 5 minutes, or a second seizure without recovery; protect from injury, do not restrain, do not put anything in the mouth, place in recovery position when able.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ILAE operational definition of status epilepticus
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Generalised convulsive activity lasting >5 minutes (t1) defines status epilepticus and mandates emergency treatment; risk of long-term neuronal injury from ~30 minutes (t2).
